authoritative_sources.title: Barbering and Cosmetology Inspections Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR is a primary state regulator for cosmetology establishments. Its inspection guide is a direct operating reference for licenses, consumer notices, blood-exposure clean-up kits, whirlpool foot-spa cleaning, and sanitation records used in spa-like establishments.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Spa context, room-readiness, sanitation, and inspection-evidence grounding

authoritative_sources.title: Establishment Forms and Publications
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR's forms page publishes the regulator-approved health and safety materials and the Foot Spa Cleaning and Disinfecting Record, so it is authoritative for the evidence artifacts a spa operation may need to retain.

authoritative_sources.title: Medical Spas
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR's medical-spa guidance is a direct authority-boundary source for when normal spa operations become medical-spa work subject to physician, nursing, or other licensed oversight.

authoritative_sources.title: Who Regulates What?
authoritative_sources.publisher: Texas Department of Licensing and Regulation
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TDLR's regulator-boundary page is an authoritative public reference for distinguishing cosmetology, massage therapy, and other regulated services that may exist inside a spa environment.

authoritative_sources.title: Recommended Cleaning and Disinfection Procedures for Foot Spa Basins in Salons
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: EPA regulates antimicrobial pesticide products. This page is the direct workflow reference for foot-spa basin draining, cleaning, and disinfection using EPA-registered products in salon and spa operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Foot-spa turnover and sanitation exception handling

authoritative_sources.title: Nail Salons
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's nail-salon resource is a direct worker-safety operating reference for salon and spa-adjacent environments, covering the standards and training posture relevant to daily service operations.

authoritative_sources.title: Nail Salons - Biological Hazards
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's biological-hazards page is a direct public control reference for blood exposure, reusable tools, and contaminated materials, which materially changes spa operations handling when incidents occur.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Incident handling and contaminated-tool boundary control

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1910.1200 - Hazard Communication
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA's Hazard Communication standard is the governing control standard for labels, safety data sheets, and training related to hazardous chemicals, making it directly relevant to spa cleaning and treatment-product handling.

authoritative_sources.title: Model Aquatic Health Code
authoritative_sources.publisher: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CDC's MAHC is the recognized public workflow reference for aquatic venues, including hot tubs and spas, which matters when a tenant's spa operation includes hydrotherapy or wet-area services.

workflow_stages.name: Spa context and booking intake grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the service type, appointment status, facility context, room assignment, tenant policy, and whether the workflow is ordinary spa work or crosses into massage, esthetics, or med-spa scope.

workflow_stages.name: Readiness and sanitation prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Verify room-turn completion, linen and tool status, foot-spa or basin cleaning records, staff assignment, and any designated contraindication-routing artifacts before service progression.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: In-process service execution and guest-impact handling
workflow_stages.description: Track booking state, room readiness, guest communications, timing, add-ons, and service-recovery posture while keeping approval and safety boundaries explicit.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Worker-safety, blood-exposure, and chemical-control handling
workflow_stages.description: Preserve OSHA-driven controls for contaminated tools, biological exposure, labels, safety data sheets, and hazardous-chemical handling instead of turning them into routine scheduling choices.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Wet-area and hydrotherapy readiness control
workflow_stages.description: Where applicable, confirm basin or hydrotherapy turnover and wet-area readiness using public sanitation and aquatic workflow references plus tenant-specific SOPs.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence and boundary-safe closure
workflow_stages.description: Close work only when booking disposition, sanitation evidence, customer-impact handling, and completion records align across the designated systems of record.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: appointment books, service menus, waitlists, arrival windows, and room assignments
guest intake, consent, waiver, allergy, and contraindication-routing records
room-turn checklists, linen logs, tool-cleaning logs, and foot-spa basin cleaning records
EPA-registered disinfectant inventories, labels, and safety data sheets
facility maintenance tickets, wet-area readiness logs, incident reports, and closure approvals
POS, CRM, guest communication logs, service recovery notes, and completion checklists
systems_of_record: document repositories for intake and consent artifacts
wet-area or hydrotherapy monitoring logs where applicable

allowed: classify spa work as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked, on hold, rework, or complete based on documented execution state and evidence
request or validate the minimum tenant facts, approved service-policy records, sanitation evidence, and systems-of-record context needed to continue safely
prepare evidence-backed workflow summaries, completion records, and escalation packages without overstating authority
escalate: medical-spa procedures, injections, or delegated medical acts
clinical or licensed-practitioner decisions about contraindications, treatment suitability, or adverse reactions
formal safety signoff, chemical-safety overrides, blood-exposure disposition, or facility-engineering decisions
refuse: advance or complete spa work when required booking, sanitation, room-readiness, or completion evidence is missing and would change the outcome
treat the spa label as cosmetic and answer with generic hospitality or office-operations assumptions
bypass blood-exposure, disinfectant-label, wet-area, or sanitation controls to preserve schedule adherence or customer convenience

domain_failure_modes: a service is allowed to continue even though room-turn or sanitation evidence is incomplete
a foot-spa basin or reusable implement is reused without the required cleaning or biological-hazard handling record
the booking system says a guest can be served while facility or maintenance records show the room or wet area is not ready
the lane drifts into med-spa, esthetics, massage-therapy, or clinical judgment because the spa label is treated too broadly
completion is declared without booking disposition, sanitation evidence, guest-impact resolution, or signoff records
chemical products are handled without label, SDS, or training context required by worker-safety controls
tenant-specific minors, consent, or turnaround rules are ignored in favor of generic service scripts

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline includes direct Texas and federal sources, but every tenant still needs jurisdiction retrieval for local cosmetology, massage-therapy, sanitation, and med-spa rules before the lane can make location-specific operating claims.

CDC MAHC becomes relevant only when the tenant operates hydrotherapy pools, hot tubs, or similar wet-area services. Tenants without those services should omit that branch at runtime rather than generalize it.

Public sources support routing and refusal boundaries, but the exact tenant workflow for who records, reviews, and clears contraindications must be supplied at runtime because it varies by service mix and jurisdiction.

notes: Authoritative records for this lane are the tenant-designated booking, intake, sanitation, incident, maintenance, and completion systems constrained by TDLR, OSHA, EPA, and CDC public baselines where applicable.

The lane stops before licensed clinical, med-spa, practitioner-scope, formal safety signoff, and legal or policy-override authority even when the guest is already booked and customer-impact pressure is high.
